title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR THE PROCESSING OF FROG LEGS CXC 30-1983

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR THE PROCESSING OF FROG LEGS CXC 30-1983
sections.summary: Document title: Code of Hygienic Practice for the Processing of Frog Legs (CXC 30-1983).

sections.section: 1. SECTION I - SCOPE
sections.summary: States the Code applies to frog legs from edible frogs and contains minimum hygiene requirements for production, processing, handling, packing, storage, transportation and distribution to ensure a healthful, wholesome supply.

sections.section: 2. SECTION II - DEFINITIONS
sections.summary: Lists definitions used in the Code for terms relevant to frog-leg production, specifically: chilling (cooling to a temperature approaching melting ice), contamination (any objectionable matter in the product), disinfection (reduction of microorganisms by hygienically acceptable chemical and/or physical methods without adversely affecting the food), establishment (building(s)/area(s) where food is handled after harvesting under the same management), and fresh frog legs (skinless hind legs of freshly killed frogs).

sections.section: 2.1 Chilling means the process of cooling to a temperature approaching that of melting ice;
sections.summary: Defines chilling as the process of cooling to a temperature approaching that of melting ice.

sections.section: 2.2 Contamination means occurrence of any objectionable matter in the product.
sections.summary: Defines contamination as the occurrence of any objectionable matter in the product.

sections.section: 2.3 Disinfection means  the  reduction,  without  adversely  affecting  the  food,  by  means  of  hygienically satisfactory chemical agents and/or physical methods, of the number of microorganisms to a level that will not lead to harmful contamination of food.
sections.summary: Defines disinfection as the reduction, without adversely affecting the food, by hygienically satisfactory chemical agents and/or physical methods of microorganisms to a level that will not lead to harmful contamination of food.

sections.section: 2.4 Establishment means  any  building(s)  or  area(s)  in  which  food  is  handled  after  harvesting  and  the surroundings under the control of the same management.
sections.summary: Defines establishment as any building(s) or area(s) in which food is handled after harvesting and the surroundings under the control of the same management.

sections.section: 2.5 Fresh frog legs means the skinless hind legs of freshly killed frogs.
sections.summary: Defines fresh frog legs as the skinless hind legs of freshly killed frogs.

sections.section: 3. SECTION III - HYGIENE REQUIREMENTS IN PRODUCTION/HARVESTING AREA
sections.summary: Sets hygiene requirements for production/harvesting areas for frog legs, including protection from contamination by wastes, supervised pest and disease control per the official agency, and protecting harvest environments against contamination that could pose a health hazard to consumers.

sections.section: 3.1 Environmental Hygiene in Areas from which Frog Legs are Obtained
sections.summary: Addresses environmental hygiene where frog legs are obtained: protect frogs from contamination by wastes, require pest/disease control measures only under qualified supervision and per official agency recommendations, and protect harvest areas from contamination posing a health hazard.

sections.section: 3.1.1 Protection from contamination by wastes .  Frogs should be protected in so far as practicable from contamination  with  human,  animal,  domestic,  industrial  and  agricultural  wastes  and  adequate  precautions should be taken to ensure that these wastes are not used or disposed of in a manner which may constitute a health hazard through the food.
sections.summary: Requires that frogs be protected, as far as practicable, from contamination by human, animal, domestic, industrial and agricultural wastes and that precautions be taken to ensure wastes are not used or disposed of in a way that could create a health hazard through the food.

sections.section: 3.1.2 Pest and disease control . Control measures involving treatment with chemical, physical or biological agents  should  only  be  undertaken  by  or  under  direct  supervision  of  personnel  who  have  a  thorough understanding of the potential hazards to health, particularly those which may arise from residues in the food. Such measures should only be carried out in accordance with the recommendations of the official agency having jurisdiction.
sections.summary: States that pest and disease control measures involving chemical, physical or biological agents must be undertaken only by or under the direct supervision of personnel who understand potential health hazards (including residues) and only in accordance with recommendations of the official agency having jurisdiction.

sections.section: 3.1.3 Harvest areas .  The environment where frogs are caught or collected should be protected in so far as practicable against contamination which may constitute a health hazard to the consumer through the product.
sections.summary: Requires that the environment where frogs are caught or collected be protected, as far as practicable, against contamination that may constitute a health hazard to consumers via the product.

sections.section: 3.2 Harvesting and Production
sections.summary: Covers hygienic harvesting and production for frog legs, requiring methods that avoid health hazards and contamination, prevent injury, dirt contamination, exposure to unfavorable temperatures and rough handling, and mandates proper equipment/container standards, segregation and approved disposal of unfit frogs, and protection from contaminants during handling and storage.

sections.section: 3.2.1 Techniques
sections.summary: Requires hygienic harvesting techniques to avoid health hazards or contamination and to prevent live frogs suffering injury/bruising, contamination with extraneous matter, exposure to unfavorable temperatures, rough handling, and to ensure harvesting under minimal stress (e.g., proper fill to avoid overloading containers).

sections.section: 3.2.2 Equipment and product containers
sections.summary: Requires equipment and containers used for harvesting to be constructed and maintained so they do not constitute a health hazard, with reusable containers permitting easy thorough cleaning and disinfection, and prohibits reuse of containers that held toxic materials for foods or food ingredients.

sections.section: 3.2.3 Removal of obviously unfit raw materials
sections.summary: Mandates segregation during collection and on arrival of unfit frogs (e.g., less active, injured, with blood clots or parasites) and their prompt removal and appropriate disposal, with removal/segregation arrangements approved by the official agency having jurisdiction.

sections.section: 3.2.4 Protection against contamination and damage
sections.summary: Requires suitable precautions to protect frogs from contamination by animals, insects, vermin, birds, chemicals, microbiological contaminants or other objectionable substances during handling and storage.

sections.section: 3.3 Storage at the Place of Production/harvesting
sections.summary: Requires live frogs kept in a sanitary environment until processing and mandates immediate removal and discard of frogs that die, become weak or appear abnormal.

sections.section: 3.4 Transportation
sections.summary: Requires conveyances for transporting harvested frogs to be adequate for purpose, constructed of materials permitting easy thorough cleaning and maintained clean and disinfected when necessary; handling must prevent contamination, keep frogs alive, minimize damage and stress, use refrigeration when distances warrant, and if ice contacts the product it must meet the specified quality (paragraph 4.4.1.2).

sections.section: 3.4.1 Conveyances for transporting the harvested frogs from the production area or place of harvest or storage should be adequate for the purpose intended and should be of such material and construction as will permit easy and thorough cleaning
sections.summary: Requires transport conveyances for harvested frogs to be adequate for purpose, made of materials and construction that permit easy thorough cleaning, and to be maintained clean and disinfected where necessary.

sections.section: ## 4. SECTION IV - ESTABLISHMENT: DESIGN AND FACILITIES
sections.summary: Section heading only: introduces Section IV on establishment design and facilities but contains no substantive requirements in the provided text.

sections.section: ## 4.1 Location
sections.summary: Establishments must be sited where they are free from objectionable odors, smoke, dust or other contaminants and are not subject to flooding.

sections.section: ## 4.3 Buildings and Facilities
sections.summary: Buildings and facilities must be sound, provide adequate working space and regulated process flow, be designed for easy cleaning and pest/environmental contaminant exclusion, separate operations that may cause cross-contamination (including separate areas/plants for non-food processing and frog‑legs by‑products), use hygienic surfaces and fittings (floors, walls, ceilings, windows, doors, chutes, overheads), segregate living quarters, control access where appropriate, avoid materials that cannot be cleaned, and provide adequate chilling, rapid‑freezing equipment and cold storage with temperature control and recording.

sections.section: ## 4.3.5 Separation of processes .
sections.summary: Buildings and facilities must provide separation (by partition, location or other effective means) between operations that could cause cross‑contamination.

sections.section: 4.3 Hygienic Facilities
sections.summary: Specifies hygienic facility requirements for buildings—sound construction, adequate space, cleanable design, pest and contaminant prevention, separation of processes, regulated process flow, hygienic surfaces and fittings, segregation from living/animal areas, access control, avoidance of uncleanable materials, and provision of appropriate chilling and freezing for frog legs.

sections.section: 4.3.1 Water supply
sections.summary: Floors in food handling areas should be waterproof, non‑absorbent, washable, non‑slip, non‑toxic, free of crevices, easy to clean and disinfect, and where appropriate sloped to drain to trapped outlets.

sections.section: 4.3.8 In food handling areas all overhead structures and fittings should be installed in such a manner as to avoid contamination directly or indirectly of food and raw materials by condensation and drip, and should not hamper cleaning operations.  They should be insulated where appropriate and be so designed and finished as to prevent the accumulation of dirt and to minimize condensation, mould development and flaking.  They should be easy to clean.
sections.summary: Overhead structures and fittings in food handling areas must be installed and finished to avoid contamination from condensation or drip, minimize dirt accumulation, condensation, mould and flaking, be insulated where appropriate, and be easy to clean.

sections.section: 4.3.9 Living  quarters,  toilets  and  areas  where  animals  are  kept should  be  completely  separated  from  and should not open directly on to food handling areas.
sections.summary: Living quarters, toilets and areas where animals are kept must be completely separated from and must not open directly onto food handling areas.

sections.section: 4.3.10 Where appropriate, establishments should be so designed that access can be controlled.
sections.summary: Establishments should be designed, where appropriate, so that access to premises and areas can be controlled.

sections.section: 4.3.11 The use of material  which  cannot be adequately cleaned and disinfected, such as wood, should be avoided unless its use would clearly not be a source of contamination.
sections.summary: Materials that cannot be adequately cleaned and disinfected (e.g., wood) should be avoided in food areas unless their use clearly would not be a source of contamination.

sections.section: 4.3.12 Adequate facilities should be available to maintain frog legs in a chilled condition, as required.
sections.summary: Adequate facilities must be available to maintain frog legs in a chilled condition as required.

sections.section: 4.3.13 Refrigeration and freezing equipment should be properly designed and constructed to accomplish rapid freezing and should be of adequate capacity.
sections.summary: Refrigeration and freezing equipment must be properly designed and constructed for rapid freezing and be of adequate capacity.

sections.section: 4.3.14 Freezer and cold storage facilities should be adequate for the intended production and should be fitted with automatic temperature controlling and recording devices.
sections.summary: Freezer and cold storage facilities must be adequate for intended production and fitted with automatic temperature control and recording devices.

sections.section: ## 4.4 Hygienic Facilities
sections.summary: An ample supply of potable water at adequate pressure and suitable temperature must be available with protected storage and distribution, and potability must meet at least the WHO "International Standards of Drinking Water."

sections.section: ## 4.4.1 Water supply
sections.summary: (Water supply requirements) An ample supply of potable water under adequate pressure and suitable temperature must be provided with protected storage and distribution, meeting at least WHO drinking‑water standards.

sections.section: 4.4.1.1  An  ample  supply  of potable  water  under  adequate  pressure  and  of  suitable  temperature  should  be available with adequate facilities for its storage, where necessary, and distribution, and with adequate protection against contamination and pollution.  The standards of potability should not be less than those contained in the latest edition of "International Standards of Drinking Water" (WHO).
sections.summary: An ample supply of potable water at adequate pressure and suitable temperature must be available with appropriate storage, distribution and protection against contamination, complying with WHO drinking‑water standards.

sections.section: 4.4.1.2 Ice should  be  made  from  potable  water  and  should  be  manufactured,  handled  and  stored  so  as  to protect it from contamination.
sections.summary: Ice must be made from potable water and manufactured, handled and stored in a manner that protects it from contamination.

sections.section: 4.4.1.3 Steam used  in  direct  contact  with  food  or  food  contact  surfaces  should  not  contain  any substances which may be hazardous to health or may contaminate the food.
sections.summary: Steam used in direct contact with food or food‑contact surfaces must not contain substances that are hazardous to health or that may contaminate the food.

sections.section: 4.4.7 Ventilation .  Adequate ventilation should be provided to prevent excessive heat, steam condensation and dust and to remove contaminated air.  The direction of the air flow should never be from a dirty area to a clean  area.    Ventilation  openings  should  be  provided  with  a  screen  or  other  protecting  enclosure  of non-corrodible material.  Screens should be easily removable for cleaning.
sections.summary: Provide adequate ventilation to prevent excessive heat, condensation and dust and to remove contaminated air, ensure air flow never goes from dirty to clean areas, and fit ventilation openings with non‑corrodible, removable screens for cleaning.

sections.section: 4.4.8 Facilities for storage of waste and inedible material . Facilities should be provided for the storage of waste and inedible material prior to removal from the establishment.  These facilities should be designed to prevent  access  to  waste  or  inedible  material  by  pests  and  to  avoid  contamination  of  food,  potable  water, equipment, buildings or roadways on the premises.
sections.summary: Provide facilities for storage of waste and inedible material prior to removal that prevent pest access and avoid contamination of food, potable water, equipment, buildings or roadways.

sections.section: 4.5 Equipment and Utensils
sections.summary: Equipment and utensils that contact food must be made of materials that do not transmit toxic substances, odor or taste, are non‑absorbent, corrosion‑resistant, withstand repeated cleaning/disinfection, have smooth surfaces free of pits and crevices, avoid contact corrosion and the use of uncleanable materials (e.g., wood) unless not a contamination risk; sanitary design and installation are required.

sections.section: 4.5.1 Materials .  All equipment and utensils used in food handling areas and which may contact food should be made of material which does not transmit toxic substances, odour or taste, is non-absorbent, is resistant to corrosion and is capable of withstanding repeated cleaning and disinfection.  Surfaces should be smooth and free  from  pits  and  crevices.    The  use  of wood and other materials which cannot be adequately cleaned and disinfected should be avoided except when their use would clearly not be a source of contamination.  The use of different materials in such a way that contact corrosion can occur should be avoided.
sections.summary: Specifies that equipment and utensils that may contact food must be made of non‑toxic, non‑odour/taste transmitting, non‑absorbent, corrosion‑resistant materials able to withstand repeated cleaning/disinfection with smooth, pit‑free surfaces; avoid wood or other materials that cannot be adequately cleaned except where they do not pose contamination risk, and avoid combinations of materials that cause contact corrosion.

sections.section: 4.5.2 Sanitary design, construction and installation
sections.summary: Requires sanitary design, construction and installation so equipment/utensils prevent hygienic hazards, allow easy thorough cleaning/disinfection and inspection, and stationary equipment permit access for cleaning; mandates leak‑proof, impervious, cleanable or disposable containers for inedible material located below the level where frog legs are processed to prevent splashback; and requires identification of equipment used for inedible materials so they are not used for edible products.

sections.section: 4.5.2.1  All equipment and utensils should be so designed and constructed as to prevent hygienic hazards and permit easy and thorough cleaning and disinfection and, where practicable, be visible for inspection.  Stationary equipment should be installed in such a manner as to permit easy access and thorough cleaning.
sections.summary: All equipment and utensils must be designed and constructed to prevent hygienic hazards, permit easy and thorough cleaning and disinfection and, where practicable, be visible for inspection, with stationary equipment installed to allow easy access for cleaning.

sections.section: 4.5.2.2 Containers  for  inedible  material  and  waste should  be  leak-proof,  constructed  of  metal  or  other suitable impervious material which should be easy to clean or disposable and be able to be closed accurately. Such  containers,  used  on  the  processing  line,  should  be  located  below  the  level  at  which  the  frog  legs  are processed and in such a way that there is no splashback on the processing line.
sections.summary: Containers for inedible material and waste must be leak‑proof, metal or other impervious, easy to clean or disposable and closable, and when used on the processing line must be located below the level where frog legs are processed and positioned to prevent splashback onto the line.

sections.section: 4.5.2 Equipment identification .  Equipment and utensils used for inedible materials should be so identified and should not be used for edible products.
sections.summary: Equipment and utensils used for inedible materials must be clearly identified and must not be used for edible products.

sections.section: 5. SECTION V - ESTABLISHMENT: HYGIENE REQUIREMENTS
sections.summary: Establishment hygiene requirements mandate maintaining buildings, equipment and facilities in good repair and orderly condition; implementing cleaning and disinfection per the Code with schedules, trained staff and a responsible person; removing by‑products and waste frequently (at least daily) and preventing pest access; cleaning/disinfecting premises and equipment after waste handling; and excluding uncontrolled animals that could be health hazards.

sections.section: 5.1 Maintenance .  The buildings, equipment, utensils and all other physical facilities of the establishment, including drains, should be maintained in good repair and in an orderly condition.  As far as practicable, rooms should be kept free from steam, vapour and surplus water.
sections.summary: Buildings, equipment, utensils and other physical facilities, including drains, must be maintained in good repair and orderly condition, and rooms should be kept free from steam, vapour and surplus water as far as practicable.

sections.section: 5.2 Cleaning  and  disinfection  should  meet  the  requirements  of  this  Code.  For  further  information  on cleaning and disinfection procedures see the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC-1969).
sections.summary: Cleaning and disinfection must meet this Code (see CXC‑1969 for procedures); equipment and utensils must be cleaned as frequently as necessary and disinfected when required, and stock solutions such as hypochlorite should be analyzed for available chlorine prior to use.

sections.section: 5.2.2 To  prevent  contamination  of  food,  all  equipment  and  utensils  should  be  cleaned  as  frequently  as necessary and disinfected whenever circumstances demand. Stock solutions such as hypochlorite should be
sections.summary: To prevent food contamination, all equipment and utensils must be cleaned as often as necessary and disinfected when circumstances demand, and chemical stock solutions (e.g., hypochlorite) must be analyzed for available chlorine before use.

sections.section: 5.2 Adequate  precautions  should  be  taken  to  prevent  food  from  being  contaminated  during  cleaning  or  disinfection  of  rooms,  equipment  or  utensils  by  water  and  detergents  or  by  disinfectants  and  their  solutions. Detergents and disinfectants should be suitable for the purpose intended and should be acceptable to the official agency having jurisdiction.  Any residues of these agents on a surface which may come in contact with food should  be removed by  thorough  rinsing  with  potable  water  before  the  area  or  equipment  is  again  used  for handling food.
sections.summary: Require precautions to prevent food contamination during cleaning/disinfection by water, detergents or disinfectants; detergents/disinfectants must be suitable and acceptable to the official agency, and any residues on food contact surfaces must be removed by thorough rinsing with potable water before reuse.

sections.section: 5.2.4 Either immediately after cessation of work for the day or at such other times as may be appropriate, floors, including drains, auxiliary structures and walls of food handling areas should be thoroughly cleaned.
sections.summary: Floors (including drains), auxiliary structures and walls of food handling areas should be thoroughly cleaned either immediately after cessation of work for the day or at other appropriate times.

sections.section: 5.2.5 Changing facilities and toilets should be kept clean at all times.
sections.summary: Changing facilities and toilets must be kept clean at all times.

sections.section: 5.2.6 Roadways and yards in the immediate vicinity of and serving the premises should be kept clean.
sections.summary: Roadways and yards in the immediate vicinity of and serving the premises should be kept clean.

sections.section: 5.3 Hygiene Control Programme . A permanent cleaning and disinfection schedule should be drawn up for each establishment to ensure that all areas are appropriately cleaned and that critical areas, equipment and material are designated for special attention.  A single individual who should preferably be a permanent member of the staff of the establishment and whose duties should be independent of production, should be appointed to be  responsible  for  the  cleanliness  of  the  establishment.    He  should  have  a  thorough  understanding  of  the significance  of  contamination  and  the  hazards involved.    All  cleaning  personnel  should  be  well-trained  in cleaning techniques.
sections.summary: Each establishment must have a permanent cleaning and disinfection schedule identifying critical areas/equipment/materials, appoint preferably a permanent, production‑independent person responsible for cleanliness who understands contamination hazards, and ensure all cleaning personnel are well trained in cleaning techniques.

sections.section: 5.4 By-Products . By-products should be stored in such a manner as to avoid contamination of food.  They should be removed from the working areas as often as necessary and at least daily.
sections.summary: By‑products must be stored to avoid contaminating food and removed from working areas as often as necessary and at least daily.

sections.section: 5.5 Storage and Disposal of Waste .  Waste  material  should  be  handled in such a manner as to avoid contamination of food or potable water.  Care should be taken to prevent access to waste by pests.  Waste should be removed from the food handling and other working areas as often as necessary and at least daily. Immediately after disposal of the waste, receptacles used for storage and any equipment which has come into contact with the waste should be cleaned and disinfected.  The waste storage area should also be cleaned and disinfected.
sections.summary: Waste must be handled to avoid contamination of food or potable water and prevent pest access, removed from food handling and working areas as often as necessary and at least daily, and after disposal all storage receptacles, equipment that contacted waste, and the waste storage area must be cleaned and disinfected.

sections.section: 5.6 Exclusion of Domestic Animals .  Animals that are uncontrolled or that could be a hazard to health should be excluded from establishments.
sections.summary: Uncontrolled animals or animals that could be a health hazard must be excluded from establishments.

sections.section: 5.7 Pest Control
sections.summary: Pest control requires an effective continuous programme with regular inspections for infestation; if pests gain entry, eradication must be instituted under supervision by personnel knowledgeable about health hazards and residue risks and in accordance with official agency recommendations, with pesticides used only when other measures fail and precautions taken to safeguard food and equipment (including cleaning equipment after application).

sections.section: 5.7.1 There should be an effective and continuous programme for the control of pests.  Establishments and surrounding areas should be regularly examined for evidence of infestation.
sections.summary: There must be an effective, continuous pest control programme and establishments and surrounding areas should be regularly examined for evidence of infestation.

sections.section: 5.7.2 Should pests gain entrance to the establishment, eradication measures should be instituted.  Control measures involving treatment with chemical, physical or biological agents should only be undertaken by or under direct supervision of personnel who have a thorough understanding of the potential hazards to health resulting from the use of these agents, including those which may arise from residues retained in the product.  Such measures should only be carried  out  in  accordance  with  the  recommendations  of  the  official  agency  having jurisdiction.
sections.summary: If pests enter the establishment, eradication measures must be instituted and any control using chemical, physical or biological agents may only be done by or under the direct supervision of personnel fully aware of health hazards and potential residues, and must follow recommendations of the official agency having jurisdiction.

sections.section: 5.7.3 Pesticides  should  only  be  used  if  other  precautionary  measures  cannot  be  used  effectively.    Before pesticides are applied, care should be taken to safeguard all food, equipment and utensils from contamination.
sections.summary: Pesticides should be used only when other precautionary measures are ineffective; before application, food, equipment and utensils must be protected from contamination, and any contaminated equipment or utensils must be thoroughly cleaned afterward to remove residues.

sections.section: 5.8 Storage of Hazardous Substances
sections.summary: Pesticides and other hazardous substances must be labeled with toxicity warnings, stored in locked rooms or cabinets and handled only by authorized or supervised trained personnel to avoid contaminating food; substances that could contaminate food must not be used or stored in food handling areas except when necessary for hygiene or processing, and personal effects and clothing must not be deposited in food handling areas.

sections.section: 6. SECTION VI - PERSONNEL HYGIENE AND HEALTH REQUIREMENTS
sections.summary: Managers must provide continuing hygiene training to food handlers; medical examinations should be arranged when required, persons with communicable disease or infected wounds must be excluded, injuries must be waterproof covered, hands washed frequently under warm potable running water with supervision and notices displayed, personnel must maintain high personal cleanliness and wear clean protective clothing, prohibited behaviours (eating, tobacco use, spitting, etc.) are banned in food areas, gloves must be kept clean and do not replace handwashing, visitors must be controlled and observe hygiene provisions, and specific supervisory responsibility for compliance must be assigned.

sections.section: 7.4.1 Only sound frogs and frog legs of good quality should be accepted for processing.
sections.summary: Only sound frogs and frog legs of good quality shall be accepted for processing.

sections.section: 7.4.2 Preparatory operations
sections.summary: Frogs must be washed to remove contamination using water that meets Sub-Section 7.3, with washing performed in running water for at least 24 hours in a clean holding tank fitted with a wire false bottom and oppositely placed inlets and outlets to remove soil, faeces and slime.

sections.section: 7.4.2.1 Washing or other preparation .  Frogs should be washed to remove any contamination.  Water used for washing and rinsing should comply with the recommendations laid down in Sub-Section 7.3.
sections.summary: Frogs should be washed to remove contamination using water that complies with Sub-Section 7.3.

sections.section: 7.4.2.2  The washing of the frogs should be done in running water for at least 24 hours in a clean holding tank, with a false bottom of wire, with a series of outlets at one side of the bottom and a series of water inlets at the upper-side opposite the side of the outlets, to remove soil, faeces and slime.
sections.summary: Washing of frogs must be done in running water for at least 24 hours in a clean holding tank with a wire false bottom and a series of outlets and opposing inlets to remove soil, faeces and slime.

sections.section: 7.4.3 Slaughter and butchering
sections.summary: Live frogs must be humanely stunned (e.g., electrically, not by immersion in 10% salt), killed immediately by severing the head or destroying the brain, and hind legs removed by a cut close to the waist without damaging the intestines, with any remaining viscera, cloaca and surrounding skin removed promptly and hygienically.

sections.section: 7.4.3.1  Before slaughtering the live frogs should be stunned, so they are relieved from pain during the cutting. This  should  be  done  in  a  humane  manner,  e.g.  by  electricity  (and  not  by  putting  them  in  a  10%  solution  of common salt).
sections.summary: This section contains only the heading '7.5 Packaging' and provides no substantive text.

sections.section: 7.4 Bleeding
sections.summary: The legs must be washed and bled immediately after cutting and immersed in chilled brine (maximum 4°C) to prevent clotting and allow thorough bleeding. If legs are to be processed immediately they should be skinned before immersion; if not, the skin should be left on to reduce contamination.

sections.section: 7.4.1 The legs should be washed and bled immediately after cutting.  They should be immersed in chilled brine (maximum 4 o C) to prevent clotting and allow thorough bleeding.
sections.summary: Legs must be washed and bled immediately after cutting and immersed in chilled brine at a maximum of 4°C to prevent clotting and ensure thorough bleeding.

sections.section: 7.4.2 If the legs are to be processed immediately after bleeding, they should be skinned before immersion in the chilled brine.
sections.summary: If legs are to be processed immediately after bleeding, they should be skinned before immersion in the chilled brine.

sections.section: 7.4.3 If  the  legs  are  not  processed  immediately  after  bleeding,  the  skin  should  be  left  on  to  reduce  the possibility of contamination of the flesh.
sections.summary: If legs are not processed immediately after bleeding, the skin should be left on to reduce the possibility of contamination of the flesh.

sections.section: ## 7.4.4 Bleeding
sections.summary: Legs must be washed and bled immediately after cutting and immersed in chilled brine (maximum 4°C) to prevent clotting and allow thorough bleeding; they should be skinned before immersion if processed immediately, otherwise the skin should be left on to reduce contamination.

sections.section: 7.4.4.1  The legs should be washed and bled immediately after cutting.  They should be immersed in chilled brine (maximum 4 o C) to prevent clotting and allow thorough bleeding.
sections.summary: Legs must be washed and bled immediately after cutting and immersed in chilled brine at no more than 4°C to prevent clotting and ensure thorough bleeding.

sections.section: 7.4.4.2  If the legs are to be processed immediately after bleeding, they should be skinned before immersion in the chilled brine.
sections.summary: If legs are to be processed immediately after bleeding, they should be skinned before immersion in the chilled brine.

sections.section: 7.4.4.3  If  the  legs  are  not  processed  immediately  after  bleeding,  the  skin  should  be  left  on  to  reduce  the possibility of contamination of the flesh.
sections.summary: If legs are not processed immediately after bleeding, the skin should be left on to reduce the possibility of contaminating the flesh.

sections.section: ## 7.4.5 Skinning and trimming
sections.summary: Skinning and foot clipping must be done on a clean board with a continuous supply of water per Sub-Section 7.4.1.8; dressed legs must be trimmed to remove membrane remnants and hanging flesh while being inspected for parasites, bruises, blood spots and other defects, and skinned/trimmed legs must be washed thoroughly in several changes of chlorinated water at 4°C as specified in Sub-Section 7.4.1.8.

sections.section: 7.4.5.1  The removal of the skin and clipping of the feet should be carried out on a clean board over which runs a continuous supply of water in compliance with Sub-Section 7.4.1.8.
sections.summary: Removal of the skin and clipping of the feet must be carried out on a clean board with a continuous flow of water in compliance with Sub-Section 7.4.1.8.

sections.section: 7.4.5.2  After skinning and clipping, the legs should be trimmed by removing remnants of embrane and hanging pieces of flesh.  During this operation, the dressed material should be carefully examined for parasites, bruises, blood spots and other defects.
sections.summary: After skinning and clipping, legs must be trimmed to remove membrane remnants and hanging pieces of flesh and carefully examined for parasites, bruises, blood spots and other defects.

sections.section: 7.4.5.3  The skinned and trimmed legs should be washed thoroughly in several changes of water at 4 o C.  This water should be chlorinated as in Sub-Section 7.4.1.8.
sections.summary: Skinned and trimmed legs must be thoroughly washed in several changes of water at 4°C, and the wash water must be chlorinated in accordance with Sub‑Section 7.4.1.8.

sections.section: ## 7.4.6 Grading
sections.summary: Grading heading stating that size grading must be carried out before packing and freezing.

sections.section: ## 7.4.7 Holding and transporting for further processing
sections.summary: Frog legs not processed immediately must be cooled to at least 4°C as soon as possible using appropriate equipment and held at that temperature until the next processing stage, and they should not be kept chilled longer than necessary nor (preferably) transported to another plant for further processing.

sections.section: 7.4.7.1  Frog legs which are not processed immediately should be cooled to at least 4 o C as soon as possible and held in this condition until the next processing stage.  Cooling (chilling) should be done in a blast chiller or other appropriate equipment.  Chill store rooms should not be used to cool the frog legs but only to maintain them chilled after cooling.
sections.summary: Frog legs awaiting further processing must be cooled to at least 4°C as soon as possible using a blast chiller or suitable equipment and held at that temperature, with chill store rooms used only to maintain chilling and not for initial cooling.

sections.section: 7.4.7.2  Frog legs should not be held chilled for longer than necessary but should proceed as soon as possible to packaging and freezing.  It is not advisable to transport frog legs, even though chilled, to another plant for further processing.
sections.summary: Frog legs should proceed to packaging and freezing as soon as possible and not be held chilled longer than necessary, and transporting chilled frog legs to another plant for further processing is not advisable.

sections.section: ## 7.5 Packaging
sections.summary: Packaging section heading with an introductory requirement that follows on appropriate storage and selection of packaging materials (text fragmentary/incomplete).

sections.section: 7.5.1 All  packaging  material  should  be  stored  in  a  clean  and  hygienic  manner.    The  material  should  be  appropriate to the product to be packed and to the expected conditions of storage and should not transmit to the
sections.summary: Requires all packaging material to be stored cleanly and hygienically and to be appropriate to the product and expected storage conditions; the sentence is truncated and incomplete.
